findings_details.title: parseFloat diverges from parseMonetaryValue for comma-formatted string values
findings_details.body: useSegmentationData.ts:101 uses `parseFloat(property.value ?? "0") || 0` while risk-layers-pane-rollup.ts uses `parseMonetaryValue`. For a comma-formatted string like "1,000,000", parseFloat returns NaN (yielding 0 via ||), while parseMonetaryValue strips non-numeric chars first and returns 1000000. The two codepaths handling TIV from the same ScheduleOfValuesPropertyLocation properties now produce different numeric results for identically-formatted input, silently undercounting TIV in the segmentation widget. Also, `parseFloat(property.value ?? "0")` passes a number (snapshot mode) where parseFloat expects a string - this works at runtime via JS coercion (parseFloat(1000000) === 1000000) but the intent is inconsistent with the fix. Fix: replace with a shared helper (or directly use parseMonetaryValue after moving it to a shared location) so both callers normalize values identically.

findings_details.title: latitude and longitude share the same Decimal->string OpenAPI spec mismatch as value
findings_details.body: The OpenAPI spec (openapi.json and api.generated.ts:8967-8970) declares latitude and longitude as string|null (with a regex pattern). At runtime, Pydantic v2 serializes Decimal as float, so latitude and longitude arrive as numbers in JSON. All current callers defensively wrap with Number() or parseFloat(String(...)), so no crash occurs today. However: (1) there is no test verifying latitude/longitude numeric tolerance; (2) PropertyMapPopup.tsx:18 does `property.latitude == null` which correctly handles both null and undefined but would fail if latitude were 0 (falsy) - though this is unlikely for a real coordinate. The root cause is that Python Decimal -> float but OpenAPI says string; all three fields (value, latitude, longitude) have this mismatch. The correct fix is to update the OpenAPI spec/generated types to reflect the actual wire format (number), or add a custom Pydantic serializer to serialize as string. Fix: either change the Pydantic model to use `str` for these fields and stringify in the service layer, or update the OpenAPI schema to use `type: number` and re-run bin/generate-api to keep TypeScript types accurate.

findings_details.title: parseMonetaryValue silently mangles scientific-notation string values
findings_details.body: The string path of parseMonetaryValue (line 61) applies `raw.replace(/[^0-9.-]/g, "")` before calling Number(). A string like "1.5e10" becomes "1.510" (the 'e' is stripped), so Number("1.510") = 1.51 instead of 15000000000. This is a pre-existing bug in the string regex that was not introduced by this commit's refactor but was not fixed either. In practice, property values are unlikely to arrive in scientific notation from the backend since Pydantic serializes Decimal as a regular float, but the string path could receive user-supplied data. Fix: change the string branch to `const parsed = Number(raw.replace(/[^0-9.,-]/g, "").replace(/,/g, ""))` and add test cases for "1.5e10" and "$1,500.00".

findings_details.title: Test correctly exercises number-typed runtime path despite TypeScript cast
findings_details.body: The new test creates `value: Number(p.value) as unknown as string`. The `as unknown as string` cast is TypeScript-only and has zero runtime effect - at runtime, value IS a number. The test therefore genuinely exercises the `typeof raw === "number"` branch in parseMonetaryValue. The cast is the idiomatic way to satisfy TypeScript strict mode while intentionally simulating a runtime type mismatch (the snapshot serialization shape). The test correctly asserts the same highConcernShare ratio as the string-typed equivalent test, proving the number path produces identical output. No issue with the test itself - this is confirming it works as intended.

findings_details.title: fitBoundsToInView name no longer describes its behavior after selection-aware extension
findings_details.body: The function now targets the intersection of viewport AND selectedPropertyIds (when non-empty), but its name says only 'in-view'. A future reader maintaining this function will expect it to be purely viewport-driven. Consider renaming to fitBoundsToActiveTarget or adding a JSDoc that documents the selection-intersection behavior.

findings_details.title: No test for selection transitioning non-empty->empty mid-debounce
findings_details.body: selectedPropertyIdsRef is always read at fire time (not schedule time). If selectedPropertyIds changes from a non-empty Set to undefined/empty during the 120ms debounce window, the fired callback will use the new (empty) ref and fit to all in-view locations rather than the selected subset. This is correct behavior by design, but it is untested. Add a test that: (1) renders with a non-empty selection, (2) triggers an overlay change to schedule the timer, (3) re-renders with selectedPropertyIds=undefined before the timer fires, (4) advances the timer and asserts fitBounds is called on all in-view locations (not just the now-gone selection).

findings_details.title: scheduleAutoFit captures obscuredLeftPx/Right by value while selectedPropertyIdsRef is read at fire time — asymmetry is correct but undocumented
findings_details.body: obscuredLeftPx and obscuredRightPx are passed as plain values to scheduleAutoFit and closed over by the setTimeout callback. selectedPropertyIdsRef.current is read at fire time (always latest). This is intentional: overlay-dimension changes are what trigger the effect, so the closed-over values are always the final debounce values; selection changes do not re-trigger the effect and are picked up via the ref. The asymmetry is correct but a comment noting WHY obscuredLeftPx/Right are passed by value (not via a ref like the locations) would prevent future maintainers from 'fixing' it by converting them to refs, which would silently break the debounce-coalescing semantics (the last scheduled call would then see the latest dims, not the dims it was scheduled with, making the padding correct but removing the guarantee that intermediate dims were cancelled).
